g.data-narration: Welcome to Verilog Data Types! This diagram covers nets, variables, vectors, and number formats essential for hardware design.
g.text: Understanding Nets, Variables, Vectors & Number Formats

g.data-narration: Data types determine how values are stored and manipulated in hardware. Choosing the wrong type leads to simulation errors and synthesis failures.
Verilog uses four logic values: 0 for low, 1 for high, x for unknown, and z for high-impedance tri-state.
Nets represent physical connections between hardware elements. They cannot store values - they continuously drive their assigned values.
g.text: Nets represent hardware wiring. They continuously drive values and
cannot store values. Used for continuous assignments and port connections.

g.data-narration: The wire is the most common net type. It connects signals and is used with continuous assignments using the assign keyword.
g.text: Most commonly used net type for signal connections.

g.data-narration: The reg type holds values until reassigned. It does NOT always mean a flip-flop - it may synthesize to combinational or sequential logic.
g.text: Holds value until reassigned. Used in always/initial blocks.
⚠️ Important: reg does NOT always mean a flip-flop!

g.data-narration: Integer is 32-bit signed for loop counters. Real is floating-point for simulation only. Time tracks simulation time in testbenches.
Vectors are multi-bit signals using bit ranges. Use [7:0] for big-endian where bit 7 is MSB. Access single bits with index or ranges with slices.
g.text: Multi-bit signals using bit ranges. [MSB:LSB] notation defines width.

g.data-narration: Arrays model memory in Verilog. Use reg [width-1:0] name [depth-1:0] for 1D memories. Access elements with array[index].
g.text: One-dimensional arrays model memory elements like RAM/ROM.

g.data-narration: Use size'basevalue format: 8'b10101010 (binary), 8'hAA (hex), 8'd170 (decimal). Underscores improve readability.
g.text: Format: <size>'<base><value> where base is b(binary), h(hex), d(decimal), o(octal)

g.data-narration: By default, reg and wire are unsigned. Use signed keyword for signed arithmetic. Integer is always signed. Mixing causes unexpected results.
g.text: reg and wire are unsigned by default. Use signed keyword for proper arithmetic.
⚠️ Integer is always signed! Use carefully in mixed expressions.

g.data-narration: Parameters define configurable constants. Use parameters for module customization. Localparam is for local constants. Defines are for global constants.
g.text: Parameters define configurable values for module customization. localparam for internal constants.

g.data-narration: Follow best practices: use meaningful names, specify widths explicitly, use parameters, choose signed appropriately, and initialize variables.
To summarize: wire for connections, reg for procedural storage, vectors for multi-bit signals, arrays for memory, and four value logic system.
